To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to make information available to public authorities, and the public generally, on the Irish origin, and meaning of placenames.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Placenames Project carries out research into the origin and meaning of placenames. This information is made available to public authorities and the general public via their website (www.placenamesni.org).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he will continue to fund the work of PlacenamesNI.org.
answer_text: Funding for the work of the Northern Ireland Placenames Project (www.placenamesni.org) was transferred from the Department of Finance to the Department for Communities in 2022. Funding is available to continue the work until Summer 2026. Future funding options are currently being assessed by my officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any assessment that has been made of the value of small rural schools.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether Artificial Intelligence is currently being used by his Department, as a means of detecting welfare (i) fraud; and (ii) error.
answer_text: My Department continues to rely on established fraud prevention and investigation methods delivered through the Benefit Security Division, including targeted investigations, risk-based scanning, and strengthened staff training. The Department will continue to monitor developments in AI and will consider future opportunities where such technologies may add value in a safe, ethical, and proportionate manner.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister why appointments to the board of the Victims and Survivors Service have not been made despite a public competition.
answer_text: We recognise the vital role of the Victims and Survivors Service in supporting people across our society who have suffered because of our past. We are grateful for the dedication of the current Board and VSS staff, and we acknowledge the importance of VSS having a permanent Chair and a full Board complement. We are currently considering how best to proceed in respect to this competition.Our focus remains on ensuring we continue to support victims and survivors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how social care needs are being addressed in Fermanagh and South Tyrone.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline any engagement he plans to have with small rural schools in relation to his Five-Year Education Budget Strategy.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their engagement with the British Government to secure a fair and adequate Executive budget.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the steps his Department has taken to simplify procurement processes.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring procurement processes are simplified and easily accessible, particularly for local businesses. The introduction of the Procurement Act in February 2025 provided additional flexibilities and officials in my department have worked to maximise those while simplifying processes. In March 2025 the Executive approved my proposals to increase the Procurement Control Limits to reflect inflation over the last five years and also to provide greater flexibilities for public bodies. These changes ensure the procurement processes used are proportionate to the level of spend while ensuring local accountability with spending decisions made locally. The new Public Procurement Policy Statement was agreed by the Executive and published in June 2025 sets out the Executive’s priorities and strategy for a more ethical, simplified and accessible procurement environment.My department also brought forward revised guidance in the format of a Dear Accounting Letter reducing red tape and simplifying tendering processes for grant recipients especially those in the VCSE sectors. This was approved by the Executive in October 2025. Additionally, revisions to the policy concerning the procurement of social and other specific services were approved by the Executive in December 2025. This has allowed the process to be simplified and focus solely on below threshold social and community-based specific services.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the review of the Disabled Facilities Grant; and (ii) whether he will consider expediting applications where the individual has a terminal illness.
answer_text: (i) My officials are progressing a review of the Disabled Facilities Grant, alongside the Housing Executive and Department of Health, as part of the wider review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants. The findings from this review will inform policy proposals which I will present for public consultation in 2026. (ii) I recognise that there are some exceptional cases for which we may consider prioritising while being mindful of the need for fairness and equity. The Housing Executive have taken forward a pilot as part of the review to trial an accelerated process for delivering adaptation provision for exceptional cases, across all housing tenures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the reason why there were only 40 Social Housing Development Programme housing starts between July 2025 and September 2025.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“New social housing starts via the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP), can only be confirmed when housing associations have secured planning approval (including confirmation of NI Water solutions as required) and project approval (confirmation of Housing Association Grant allocation); and have acquired land/property and entered into a purchase contract/works contract/development agreement for each new social housing scheme proposal. Delivery of SHDP is often backloaded within a given programme year, which is typically a reflection of the time required to confirm the steps above, in particular the timeframe to secure planning approval. It is not atypical that a relatively low number of new social housing starts is confirmed in the period July-September (Q2).In the 2025/26 year, the opening SHDP budget allocation was not confirmed until June 2025. This delayed progress with the issuing of new project approvals to housing associations. By the end of September 2025, 12 project approvals were in place with an associated 152 social homes. However, several of these proposals have been delayed reaching start stage, primarily due to outstanding planning or NI Water connection issues to be resolved; or legal matters relating to the land conveyancing or works contract/development agreement.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the award of £150,000 to Mid and East Antrim Borough Council for festive lighting, whether his Department carried out an equality impact assessment on the decision.
answer_text: AnswerAn equality impact assessment was not necessary as this was a routine funding decision under the Departments’ Public Realm programme. A Contract for Funding was issued to the Council which requires compliance with equality legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail under what scheme did his Department award Mid and East Antrim Borough Council £150,000 of funding for festive lighting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to the award of £150,000 to Mid and East Antrim Borough Council for festive lighting, whether the funding was awarded after an open competition which was open to other local councils to apply.

To ask the Minister for Communities to outline whether the recent safety alert announced by the Competition and Consumer Protection Commission in the South of Ireland, relating to circulating pumps installed in heating systems, manufactured by Tuscon pumps, will have any impact here.
answer_text: The issue of consumer protection in Northern Ireland is not a matter for my Department. However, I am aware that the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) have issued a Product Safety Alert for circulating pumps installed in heating systems, including those with oil and gas boilers - https://www.hseni.gov.uk/alert/hseni-safety-alert-tucson-5m-6m-and-8m-circulating-pumps-manufactured-between-2017-and-2024.I would refer the member to the HSENI and the Department for Economy for further information on the impact of this product alert.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to review how minor works funding is prioritised.
answer_text: Due to the current capital budget constraints, the minor works budget is largely directed to keeping schools open and pupils safe. Minor Works projects are restricted to Ministerial Priority 1 i.e. those works that meet inescapable statutory requirements, such as health and safety, fire protection, safeguarding and statutory obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act. Projects which meet these inescapable requirements are prioritised for delivery, although the timescales for delivery will vary depending on issues like: Their state of readiness e.g. need for planning approval.The logistics of delivery e.g. some projects can only be delivered when pupils are not on site, limiting the window to holiday periods, typically the summer months. The availability of funding e.g. some small-scale projects can be “mopped up” at the end of the financial year if there is available funding. Unlike Major Works or School Enhancement Programme projects, Minor Works projects are initiated on an almost daily basis to address inescapable concerns. The Minor Works programme therefore needs to include a degree of flexibility to respond to emerging priorities. I will continue to make the case for increased and sustained investment in our education estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is considering reintroducing the Urban Development Grant Scheme.
answer_text: Whilst my Department will not be launching an open call process under the Urban Development Grant (UDG) scheme, it will be making UDG investments with a greater strategic emphasis and in conjunction with other regeneration schemes, in line with a previous evaluation of the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will bring forward a retrofit strategy, aimed at delivering the decarbonisation of all domestic properties.
answer_text: Retrofitting, which is a cross-cutting matter, plays a key role in improving energy efficiency and reducing the carbon emissions from our homes.As residential buildings sector lead for the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027, which includes retrofitting policies and proposals, my officials have recently commissioned a high-level modelling of emission reductions to inform a long-term strategy/roadmap for the decarbonisation of the residential sector.  This will guide future policy on retrofit, energy standards, emissions reductions and household support in line with just transition principles and in collaboration with other Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the date the trilingual departmental logo was changed; (ii) the reasons for this decision; and (iii) the total cost of implementing this change.
answer_text: 25 June 2025.I asked for the logo to be changed and received information and design options from my officials on the suite of Departmental logos. It is not possible to quantity potential future savings - the guidance is applied as part of the normal replacement cycles

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider bringing forward legislation which will place a duty on the Housing Executive to prevent homelessness.
answer_text: I have not made any commitment to specific legislative changes at this time but my officials continue to engage with the Housing Executive and stakeholders in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when he will bring the Anti-Poverty Strategy back to the Executive for final approval.
answer_text: The Public Consultation on the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy closed on 19 September and my officials are currently analysing and considering all responses received. Once this is completed, I will share the outcome of this analysis with my Executive colleagues, and we will take forward any further development as appropriate before finalising the Strategy for Executive consideration and approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide the rational as to why his Department did not participate in this year's Social Welfare Summer School, alongside the Department of Social Protection.
answer_text: In 2024, following careful consideration, the Senior Leadership Team in the Department for Communities decided not to proceed with participation in Summer School. This decision was taken due to a number of factors including the broad remit of this Department outside of social welfare, the rising costs of a week-long residential and the need for a fresh approach to policy training needs.Whilst my Department no longer sends staff to participate in the full Summer School programme, six officials attended the thematic Study Group sessions in August at Dublin City University, providing them with an opportunity to liaise with their Department of Social Protection counterparts and to discuss current social policy initiatives.The Department for Communities remains keen to explore alternative forms of engagement and to enhance some of the existing collaborative forums and linkages in place between the Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when he will publish the details of the Fuel Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I expect to receive the final Strategy document in the coming weeks. Subject to my clearance, I will bring the strategy to the Executive for approval before year end.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of new language guidance published by his department, to outline the rationale for this English-only policy given his Ministerial responsibility for developing an Irish language strategy.
answer_text: My Department recently published updated Corporate Identity guidelines which include all versions of the Departmental logo including a single language logo, an abbreviated logo, and a refreshed trilingual language logo which is used when correspondence or the subject relates to Irish or Ulster-Scots language, identity or culture.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Local Change Fund.
answer_text: We are determined to do everything we can to stop the harm and abuse faced by women and girls. We have allocated funding of over £2 million to help Councils set up grant schemes and develop local responses which raise awareness and build momentum in communities.All 11 Councils have opened a Local Change Fund to support grassroots community and voluntary organisations. Activity is spread across all areas of communities including youth groups, sports organisations (boxing, GAA, IFA), schools, businesses and the arts sector. This will enable over 100 grassroots organisations to reach around 48,000 people from different areas and backgrounds.The Momentum Fund has supported 173 Council-led events, impacting over 6,500 people. We remain committed to doing everything we can to create a society where women and girls are free from all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on improving the services for young people with special educational needs.
answer_text: My Statement to the Assembly in June outlined my proposed reforms which, subject to the necessary investment, will improve and expand my Department’s pathways and supports for school leavers with special educational needs. My officials are working through the scoping and planning for this significant programme, as well as making progress with early actions. The interventions required are much wider than my Department’s remit and, for that reason, I had also indicated my intention to write to the Executive on those wider issues. I have done this, with the paper discussed and my recommendations endorsed at our Executive meeting on Thursday 18th. Consequently, these important issues will now feature as a regular item on the Executive’s agenda.Securing funding for new and enhanced provision is crucial. My officials have led on the development of a collaborative bid to the Transformation Fund, in partnership with the Departments of Health, Communities, Education and Infrastructure.  Given the priority I attach to this work, I have ensured it is reflected in my Department’s Spending Review bids.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any support his Department has provided to families that have been the victims of sectarian and racist intimidation in North Belfast.
answer_text: My officials continue to keep in contact with the relevant statutory partners supporting victims of the recent intimidation in North Belfast, including the Housing Executive and the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), as well the Housing Association whose tenants have been threatened. My Department works closely with other Executive Departments on these issues, including through the Community Safety Network. My Department, along with the PSNI, the Department of Justice, and the Housing Executive, operates the Hate Incident Practical Action Scheme. The purpose of this Scheme is to provide personal and home protection measures to victims of hate crime incidents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the criteria for search and rescue groups to be included on the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue register.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 12 - AQO 2367/22-27		  ANSWER The criteria for inclusion on the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue (NISAR) register are based on capacity and capability. Groups must demonstrate that they can operate to the highest recognised standards and that their inclusion would add value to the existing search and rescue framework. Where there is a gap in service provision or an opportunity to enhance resilience through new technology, specialist skills, or innovative approaches, applications are considered to ensure that overall capability is strengthened rather than duplicated.  Also, search and rescue groups on the NISAR Register should be accredited by their parent or governing body, or to a standard deemed suitable by a tasking authority.  Finally, voluntary organisations should have, or be working towards, UK charitable status or as deemed appropriate by the NISAR Policy Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when he will bring forward an updated Tenant Participation Strategy.
answer_text: Due to the on-going budget and resource pressures within the Department, I am unable to give a time frame for when a review of the Strategy will be resumed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline how the Delivery Unit will work across departments on key Executive priorities.
answer_text: We have established a Delivery Unit to drive reform and transformation across government and wider public services. Working across departments, the Unit will bring fresh thinking and new ways of working to ensure the best use of available resources. Focusing on a small number of the Executive’s top priorities at any one time, the Unit will provide the scrutiny, support and attention required to make the greatest possible impact on people’s lives. The Executive has agreed that the Delivery Unit will focus on our shared goal of reducing waiting times. We are working with the Health Minister to identify how the Unit can complement and add value to the work his department is taking forward in this area. In addition, all Ministers have been invited to suggest other priority areas where we can help to drive change and improve the efficiency, effectiveness and sustainability of public services. There is no “one size fits all” approach to how the Delivery Unit will work across departments—it will be flexible and responsive to the specific issues it is working on. However, based on learning from elsewhere, this work will be underpinned by effective partnership working, to bring a shared approach to problem solving.  Over time we want to see a greater focus on delivery, at pace, across all departments, to achieve positive outcomes for everyone living here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) what public realm scheme projects were considered prior to the 2025/26 budget allocations; and (ii) what criteria was used to select the public realm scheme projects which his Department allocated funding for.
answer_text: My Department considers a number of public realm priorities on an ongoing basis. These public realm schemes are worked up alongside key stakeholders (including Councils, DfI) and progressed through the necessary business case and statutory approval stages.  In 2025/26 budget allocations have been made against those public realm projects that:Have been committed to contractually from a previous year or are a previous Executive commitment; orHave all necessary business case and statutory approvals in place or are nearing approval stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans to reduce the number of Jobs and Benefits offices, as part of the rationalisation of the office estate for the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service Office Estate includes 33 Jobs and Benefits Offices (JBOs) used exclusively by the Department for Communities (DfC). These JBOs are ‘out of scope’ of the NICS Office Estate Strategy as their specific locations and any ability to rationalise these are determined by DfC. My Department works closely with DfC on its future demand for office accommodation which will be progressed in parallel with the NICS Office Estate Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail an update on the progress of the Heritage, Culture and Creativity programme.
answer_text: I launched the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme in July 2024 to deliver four new policies for public libraries, museums, the arts and the historic environment.  Significant progress has been made since I announced the programme, with a comprehensive round of engagement across local government and executive departments having been completed.The focus has now moved onto policy drafting and planning for public consultation. On current timings, it is anticipated that, allowing for public consultation processes, all the policies in this first phase of the Programme will be in place by spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he is giving to carrying out a review of current legislation on homelessness, with a view to updating our laws to reflect the ongoing homelessness crisis.
answer_text: Whilst I have not made any commitment to review the legal duties owed to individuals experiencing homelessness, I am aware that there have been suggestions of discrete changes to the underpinning legislation to better support our policy aims.My officials continue to engage with the Housing Executive and stakeholders in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how many people currently in receipt of Personal Independence Payment will be affected by the proposed changes, as outlined in the UK government's Green Paper Pathways to Work Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I provided for AQW 28403/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how regional balance has been considered within the office estate strategy for the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: The Civil Service has implemented a Hybrid Working policy which helps support regional balance by affording its workforce more choice about their working location allowing a mix of workplace, home, and remote working. As a major employer, the Civil Service recognises the potential benefits of hybrid working in contributing to regional balance; therefore, hybrid working is available, where possible, for all employment opportunities offered by the Civil Service.In addition, six Connect 2 hubs allow staff to work remotely at six regional locations, Ballykelly, Ballymena, Bangor, Craigavon, Downpatrick, Omagh as an occasional alternative to working from home or in their designated workplace.Civil Service staff are posted in accommodation across the region, both in the Department of Finance’s Office Estate and in buildings owned or leased by other departments. Decisions about where Civil Servants are located are for departments to consider in line with their business needs. Individual departments determine the number, type, and grade of posts at each location.Consolidation under the NICS Office Estate Strategy would seek to retain posts within their current geographic area unless otherwise determined by their individual department. All consolidation projects include engagement with relevant departments, staff in the buildings and appropriate consultation with Trade Union representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he is taking to ensure that rent levels in Housing Association properties are affordable, particularly given the financial pressures that renters are facing.
answer_text: My Department has no legislative power to control annual rent increases applied by Registered Housing Associations (RHAs). It is a matter for individual Housing Association’s Boards to determine appropriate rent levels and to consider and approve any rent increases. 	All Housing Associations provide welfare advice, signposting to other support services where necessary, and proactively work to identify those who are experiencing difficulties in order to provide support to ensure that tenancies continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the rationale behind her decision not to formally recognise the K9 Search and Rescue volunteer organisation, by refusing their application to be included on the Search and Rescue register.
answer_text: The decision not to accept K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland into the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue (NISAR) formal arrangements was made by the NISAR policy group and informed by the views of statutory organisations. However, I am satisfied that the decision regarding their application was made in accordance with the correct and established process based on an assessment of need, and against the agreed criteria as outlined in the application process.I am aware that K9 Search and Rescue have recently made a final appeal to myself regarding their application being declined.  This appeal is currently being considered and K9 Search and Rescue will be advised of the outcome of that decision in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on work for a permanent memorial to victims and survivors, as recommended by the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry report.
answer_text: Following engagement with victims’ and survivors’ groups, there is support for fulfilling the Inquiry recommendation through a memorial plaque in Parliament Buildings. We sought and received the Speaker’s formal approval, enabling us to proceed and continue engagement with survivors. Some survivors have expressed interest in localised memorials. Officials are exploring this with local authorities and have invited input from representative groups and those not affiliated with a group. Once potential locations are identified, we will work with the Arts Council to ensure survivors’ voices shape the final design

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the last year for which information is available, to detail the number of people that died who had both been assessed as having Limited Capability for Work and Work-Related Activity under the Normal Rules and received a Daily Living component of Personal Independence Payment, but received less than four points for each Daily Living activity in their last assessment.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the last year for which information is available, to detail the number of claims for Personal Independence Payment that were stopped due to the death of the claimant, in which the claimant received a Daily Living component but received less than four points for each Daily Living activity in their last assessment.
answer_text: My Department does not hold the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the last year for which information is available, to detail the number of people receiving Universal Credit that died that had been assessed as having Limited Capability for Work and Work-Related Activity (i) in total; and (ii) under the Special Rules for Terminal Illness.
answer_text: The relevant data is not available to enable this analysis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has given any consideration to setting up an independent body to monitor and regulate private, social and housing association service charges.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to set up such a body to monitor and regulate service charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to reform home ownership by introducing a ban on new leasehold flats, as is currently being proposed in England and Wales.
answer_text: I have noted that the British Government has produced a White Paper on commonhold. My officials are considering that document along with other material in their ongoing scoping exercise into shared property, but we must be careful not to import solutions here to problems that have arisen in another jurisdiction. I understand that the leasehold system for flats in England and Wales has thrown up problems which do not exist to the same or to any extent here. For example, leases here typically tend to be for longer terms, often 999 years, whereas in England and Wales, many problems have arisen due to the much shorter nature of leases, often 125 years. Also in England, the ownership of common areas and structures of apartment buildings tends to remain with the landlord, with individual flat owners having no ownership rights. This is not the case here because common areas of such buildings are owned by the flat owners through the management company structure. It is interesting to note that the voluntary commonhold scheme introduced in England and Wales in 2004 had a very low uptake. Likewise, the NI Law Commission Report on Apartments of May 2013 noted that the overwhelming majority of respondents were not in favour of the introduction of a system akin to commonhold in this jurisdiction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Minor Works application submitted by St. Macartan's Primary School, Clogher.
answer_text: The minor works application for an extended multi-purpose hall for St Macartan’s Primary School remains on hold due to budget constraints. I appreciate that the school would derive significant benefit from an extended multi-purpose hall which could be used for meals, PE and other curricular activities. However, I can only do so much with the budget made available to me and unfortunately, that means there is simply insufficient budget to enable the project at St Macartan’s Primary School to progress to tender and construction at the current time. I will continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained investment in our education system to ensure the capital funding allocated to education impacts positively on as many children and young people as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when letters of offer will be issued to local councils for the Community Support Programme.
answer_text: My Department has been advised of its draft 2025-26 budget allocation and the NI Executive Draft Budget is currently subject to consultation.My officials have commenced work on options to live within a further year’s constrained Budget.  An Equality Impact Assessment on the Department’s 2025-26 Budget Allocation will be drafted and published for consultation to inform Final 2025-26 Budget allocations.District councils will receive communications on Community Support Programme funding for 2025-26 by the end of March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts to ensure a paediatric pathology service is locally accessible to parents.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has at present to support the regeneration of Dungannon.
answer_text: My Department is currently providing funding through an Urban Development Grant of £915,000 to Perry Street in Dungannon.   Perry Street was previously a vacant street of derelict and underutilised buildings, and through the support of my Department, there will be nine townhouses, four apartments and three commercial units developed.  My Department is also supporting a £374,000 town centre revitalisation scheme in collaboration with Mid Ulster District Council across four urban towns in the Mid Ulster area to include Dungannon.My Departmental officials will continue to work in collaboration with Mid Ulster District Council regarding future regeneration plans for Dungannon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the creation of an Office for Building Safety, as recommended by the Expert Panel for the Building Safety Programme.  .
answer_text: As recommended by the Expert Panel, the Residential Building Safety Division was established in January 2024. The team are currently developing policy to improve residential building safety in line with the Expert Panel’s recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the statutory review of liquor licensing laws; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to phase out the surrender principle.
answer_text: The report of the independent review of the licensing system including the surrender principle was published on 21 February 2025. I have asked my officials to consider carefully the report and to provide me with advice on each of the 26 recommendations.In accordance with section 23 of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) (NI) Act 2021, my intention is to publish a plan setting out how I will respond to the report by 20 August 2025, and I will make an oral statement about my plan to the Assembly thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to introduce a levy on the gambling industry before the end of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: I am committed to progressing a Northern Ireland gambling industry levy.  My officials are currently gathering the data required to explore how this could be achieved, and are engaging with the Department of Health to make sure that a levy is progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on work to ensure the safety of disabled residents in high rise residential buildings in light of the Grenfell Tower inquiry.
answer_text: The Residential Building Safety Division (RSBD) are in the process of developing policy for building safety for High Rise Residential Buildings in Northern Ireland.  The NIHE Board approved the establishment of a Building Safety Team in November 2021 in anticipation of new statutory duties being applied in Northern Ireland for High-Risk Residential Buildings (HRRB’s) under a Northern Ireland Building Safety Act.  Fire risk assessments have been completed for all NIHE HRRB’s.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the delivery of an Irish Language Strategy.
answer_text: The Irish Language Strategy, alongside the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy, is being developed through a co-design process, in which my department is taking a lead on behalf of the Executive. The co-design group for the strategy has largely completed its current round of work on the draft strategy narrative and resulting action plan proposals.The drafts of the strategy and action plan have been shared with the Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG) of senior officials nominated by Executive Ministers. A key role of the CDWG is to determine the budgetary consequences of proposed actions, alongside considering what work already underway will allow completion of actions (either in the way envisaged by the co-design group or through another route). This work is intended to provide a strategy and action plan that can be provided to the Executive for consideration. Public consultation will follow Executive consideration and approval of the drafts. The detailed timing for this work is to a large degree outside the Department’s control, recognising the range of work priorities across Departments. Alongside the work of this Cross-Departmental Working Group I have been meeting with officials to consider the next steps in the development of both language strategies. I anticipate that the next stage will be for me to bring a paper to the Executive to ask them to consider and, if content, agree on next steps.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the lessons to be learnt from the response to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The NI Civil Contingencies Framework outlines the procedures for efficient emergency management, including preparation, response, and recovery from emergencies.Civil contingency structures were put in place before Storm Éowyn and remained operational throughout the response and recovery phases.The Recovery Coordination Group was officially stood down on 4th February. In the coming weeks, a comprehensive debriefing will be conducted involving multi-agency partners and key stakeholders to identify lessons learned. This is common practice after all major incidents and emergencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any involvement his Department had in the allocation of £710,000 to Lisneal College.
answer_text: None.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of the £710,000 allocation to Lisneal College, including how much of the allocation was earmarked for (i) repair work following a health and safety inspection; and (ii) improvements.
answer_text: There was no allocation to Lisneal College for works to the pitch. The scheme to replace the 3G pitch surface at the school will be funded from the Education Authority’s (EA) Other Capital/Minor Works budget.The EA has advised that the breakdown of the £709,500 pre-tender construction cost estimate was:Minimum Repair Works - £536,500Improvement Works - £63,500Main Contractor Preliminaries - £45,000 Model Compensation Event - £64,500Whilst it is not possible to confirm the actual total cost of the project until it is finally handed over from the contractor and the final account agreed, the EA estimates that the total project cost will be approximately £600k. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a timeline of all actions his Department has taken in relation to improving dining facilities at St Macartan's Primary School, Clogher.
answer_text: St Macartan’s Primary School submitted applications under the October 2017 Minor Works Call for refurbishment works to the school meals servery and to address the inadequate size of the school meals dining hall and stores. The works were shortlisted to be taken forward by the Education Authority (EA) under the Strategic Investment in School Meals Accommodation (SISMA) programme.In May 2021, it was agreed that the Department would contribute the additional construction cost to the SISMA scheme to deliver a 160m2 dining /multi-purpose hall, instead of the 80m2 dining facility.The Business Case was approved in April 2022 at a total cost of £1.4m and in March 2023 approval was given to go out to tender. However, in October 2023, the EA informed the Department that the project had been paused due to budget and priorities.The EA has confirmed that this project remains paused due to a very constrained capital budget position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s minor works schemes.
answer_text: As you will be aware, my Department is operating within an extremely constrained capital budget and decisions have necessarily been taken to direct available investment to keep schools open, pupils safe and provide education access for the most vulnerable. £63 million is currenly allocated for Minor Works across a schools’ estate of over 1,100 schools. That falls far short of what is required to deliver all the minor works schemes required across the estate. Due to these constraints, the minor works programme has had to be prioritised to deliver schemes which address the most urgent issues or mitigate significant health and safety risks. Schemes to ensure statutory compliance or enable implementation of an approved development proposal are also being progressed. The schools’ estate is in need of significantly increased and sustained funding to ensure every child can attend a school which has modern, safe, comfortable and suitable accommodation to help them discover their full potential. I will continue to strongly advocate for increased capital funding for education to deliver a schools’ estate which meets all requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the connection to the wastewater system for the Apex housing development at Oaks Road, Dungannon.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The development at Oaks Road, Dungannon, is a 29 dwelling social housing development being constructed by Radius Housing, rather than Apex Housing, and it is assumed that this is the development to which you refer. The original planning application for this development included for an onsite wastewater treatment plant (on the basis Dungannon WwTW was closed at the time of application). Works at Dungannon WwTW was committed in Summer 2024. The developer has subsequently decided to change the approach and apply for a connection to the WW system – this would allow the developer to build additional properties on the site of the proposed wastewater treatment works. NI Water agreed to the request subject to the requisition of new sewers to connect to the development. Sewer requisitions for storm and foul are required to connect the development which are complicated by poor ground conditions and land access issues. Our framework Contractor is prepared to mobilise upon financial conditions being met.  The site could be connected in c.14 weeks, if all financial conditions are met by the developer in advance  NI Water met with the developer’s agent on 27 November 2024, to discuss the storm sewer requisition, to service the development.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the Women's Centre Childcare Fund, including (i) the current allocations for each centre; (ii) the criteria used to allocate the funding; and (iii) any plans to review the funding in the future.
answer_text: My Department’s Women’s Centre Childcare Fund (WCCF) currently provides funding to 13 Centres across Northern Ireland, enabling access to affordable or free childcare sessions to help parents access employment and training opportunities.  In this financial year, I have allocated a 5% uplift across my Department’s Voluntary & Community Sector funding programmes, bringing the total WCCF funding to almost £881k.The 2024/25 funding allocation for each Centre is:Atlas Women's Centre£ 53,528.54Ballybeen Women's Centre£ 200,182.22Chrysalis Women's Centre£ 21,939.84Falls Women's Centre£ 59,574.22First Steps Women's Group£ 39,735.86Footprints Women's Centre £ 115,403.87Greenway Women's Group£ 70,763.34Kilcooley Women's Centre£ 41,251.92Shankill Women's Centre£ 54,054.80Strathfoyle women's Centre£ 14,060.09Waterside Women's Centre£ 41,300.49Windsor Women's Centre£ 132,523.14Women's Centre Derry£ 36,562.11	The WCCF is provided to organisations which:provide support and services to disadvantaged communities through childcare;deliver quality childcare in line with good practice and standards set by Government departments;address the specific needs of the community that the organisation serves;increase community cohesion and capacity; andstrengthen community infrastructure, community participation and development.A review of the funding is not currently scheduled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many applicants have applied to the cross-border reimbursement scheme since it opened; (ii) how many patients were approved; and (iii) how many patients have successfully received reimbursement.
answer_text: 4412 applicants applied to use the temporary ROI cross border reimbursement scheme in the period 1 July 2021 to 21 September 2022. At 30 September 2022 3667 patients were approved.At 30 September 2022 1605 patients were reimbursed.  A further 494 claims have been received and will be processed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has conducted an assessment of the expected impact on the viability of dental practices stemming from the planned reduction in the Rebuilding Support Scheme uplift from 25 per cent to 10 per cent.
answer_text: The General Dental Services Rebuilding Support Scheme was introduced in April 2022 to support dental practitioners with the impact of the pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past few months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing support has also reduced. However, it is acknowledged that the impact has not entirely receded with inefficiencies created by increased “Did Not Attends” and associated staff absences. The effect of these inefficiencies is assessed to be a 10% reduction in the GDS capacity. It is for this reason that I committed to continuing support to the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will reconsider the planned reduction in the Rebuilding Support Scheme uplift from 25 per cent to 10 per cent, in light of concerns raised by the British Dental Association.
answer_text: All elements of general support to General Dental Services (GDS) are under ongoing review.The scheme was introduced in April 2022 to support dental practitioners with the impact of the pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past few months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing support has also reduced. However, it is acknowledged that the impacts have not entirely receded with inefficiencies created by increased “Did Not Attends” and associated staff absences. The impact of these inefficiencies is assessed to be a 10% reduction in the GDS capacity. It is for this reason that I committed to continuing support to the end of the financial year.  There are no current plans to review the Rebuilding Support Scheme until the end of 2022/23

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 2178/22-27, does the range of options to support the recruitment and retention of GPs cited include options to amend the rules governing the primary medical performers list regarding GPs qualified in the south of Ireland.
answer_text: To practice as a GP in Northern Ireland, doctors are required to be on the NI Primary Medical Performers List (PMPL).  This is to ensure that GPs working in NI have the appropriate knowledge and skills to work safely. Entry to the Performers List is automatic following completion of training as a GP in the UK. If training is completed outside the UK, or if a GP has not practiced in the UK for more than 2 years, they are required to undergo the GP Induction and Refresher (I&R) Scheme.  The I&R Scheme application process includes an initial knowledge assessment (MCQ) exam before the inductee undertakes placement in a GP practice, the length of which depends on the educational needs of the individual.  At this stage the applicant is conditionally included in the Performers List. Successful completion of the practice placement leads to full inclusion.The GP Induction and Refresher Scheme already allows for GPs qualified in the South to access entry to the NI PMPL. My Department has considered the potential to amend the Scheme to provide for a more streamlined process that recognises the similarities between GP training in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland and also between the Membership of the Irish College of General Practitioners (MICGP) and the Royal College of General Practitioners (MRCGP) examination. Accordingly, I have recently approved changes to the GP Induction   Scheme so that doctors who have completed training in the Republic of Ireland will not be required to sit the knowledge test (MCQ) if they have successfully completed the Irish College of General Practitioners examination within the last 2 years from the date of their application to the Northern Ireland Performers List.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department has undertaken to develop and implement an updated Oral Health Strategy.
answer_text: The current Northern Ireland Oral Health Strategy (OHS) was published in 2007 and, despite its age, the main oral health problems described in the document and the approaches to prevention advocated by it remain valid today.Prior to the Covid 19 pandemic my Department decided that the two sections of the Northern Ireland population that would benefit most from updated oral disease prevention programs where children and the older population. Unfortunately, the pandemic delayed the establishment of the two planned working groups, but significant progress has been made by two oral health groups - one dedicated to improving the oral health of children and the other specifically looking at improving the oral health of older people.The challenges facing the HSC and DoH today mean that, unfortunately, the significant resources required to develop a new wide-ranging OHS are not available at this time. However, the importance of analysing the oral health status of the population and recommending evidence-based approaches to health improvement is recognised. In addition, my Department has funded a dental health survey of five year old children this financial year, and a survey of twelve year old children is planned for 2022/23.  These surveys will provide key information for future planning of oral health improvement in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) if he will introduce a local pharmaceutical pricing index; and (ii) what measures has he taken to reduce the costs of medicines to ensure that community pharmacists are not out having to prescribe below cost.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to providing fair and reasonable remuneration for community pharmacy contractors through the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff while ensuring that fees paid for products supplied under Drug Tariff arrangements represent good value for money for the HSC and taxpayers.My Department is continuing to work with Community Pharmacy NI, the representative body for community pharmacy, on a programme of Drug Tariff reform aimed at ensuring that the Drug Tariff continues to ensure fair and reasonable remuneration to community pharmacy contractors. Ongoing work to enhance the Drug Tariff is focussing on the continued development of the infrastructure to support an effective Drug Tariff to include continued refinement of the margin survey; the implementation of an Intelligence Unit to monitor and support the management of the supply chain; and enhancements to Business Service Organisation processes to fully implement improved arrangements. In addition active consideration is being given to what additional shorter term interventions can be made to reflect purchases made in excess of Drug Tariff prices under the existing concessionary pricing system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many graduate nurses have been employed by agencies since graduation in 2021; and (ii) how many graduate nurses have been employed across all Trusts since graduation in 2021.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information in regard to nurses employed by agencies. Normally, at least 6 months post registration experience is required for nurses before they can apply for Agency employment. I can however confirm that 794 nursing students graduated from commissioned Department of Health programmes at local universities in 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will reinstate the Cross Border Health Directive, or an alternative, to ensure that those using this service continue to have their medical needs met.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) what discussions he has had with the British Treasury Department on GP pension contributions; and (ii) whether his Department has investigated the scheme currently being set up in Wales.
answer_text: My Department is in the process of updating pension contribution bands for all members of the HSC Pension Scheme. I am not aware of any new initiative in Wales however guidance was issued to employers in Wales earlier this year in relation to pension flexibilities for those negatively impacted by pension tax. Work is underway to introduce similar guidance locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what percentage of staff leaving health and social care employment have received a leavers/exit interview.
answer_text: Where exit interviews are carried this is completed by line management and this information is stored locally. As this data is not stored centrally, it is not possible to provide a percentage figure for how many leaving staff members have received an exit interview across the Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of work on a GP indemnity scheme.
answer_text: Work is continuing to review arrangements for the provision of indemnity cover for GPs in Northern Ireland.  A range of options are under consideration from the status quo to alternatives such as the state backed systems adopted in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of current capacity in day care centres, broken down by Trust area.
answer_text: In February 2022, the Department of Health wrote to Trusts to outline my decision to approve the Public Health Agency Pathway to remobilise adult day-care, short breaks and transport. As of May 2022, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) receive fortnightly updates from each Trust on progress towards the full remobilisation of adult day care, short breaks and transport against the published plans. Data across all relevant Programmes of Care is collated by SPPG to assist with the development of a regional consistent approach. The data across all Programmes of Care is analysed and the data for Learning Disability in the first instance is published on the Department of Health website.  HSCTs continue to have work to do to reach full remobilisation. SPPG has an ongoing schedule of performance management meetings with Trusts to address the continuing areas of underperformance.  A percentage of service users and carers have chosen not to return to day care for a variety of reasons. Trusts continue to engage with those  service users who have chosen not to do this to ensure their needs continue to be met through alternative service pathways, such as day opportunities whilst advising that the option of day care remains available to them. Capacity in adult day care is measured against the Trust performance prior to Covid and refers to the number of attendances within adult day centres.  Performance in August 2022 is also presented below:February 2020August 2022VarianceBHSCT16,44212,109-4,333NHSCT12,92013,877957SEHSCT9,0026,440-2,562SHSCT8,5576,162-2,395WHSCT10,8567,746-3,110The focus is upon rebuilding adult day care services back to their pre-pandemic levels as a matter of priority. Trusts are progressing work in this regard and adult day care centres that were previously closed have now re-opened and the number of people in attendance is increasing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the RQIA report on the Expert Review of Records of Deceased Patients of Dr Watt will be published.
answer_text: The Department of Health has received the overall final detailed reports for Phase Two of the Expert Review of Records of Deceased Patients of Dr Watt, from the RQIA. The RQIA has shared individual case summary reports with the families who have engaged with Phase Two of this review and the RQIA Family Liaison Team has put in place a range of options for families to receive feedback on their individual reports alongside family support arrangements, including independent counselling services, for families who wish to avail of this.  It is appropriate that the families are provided with the space and time they require to engage with the RQIA and to consider the findings. The Department and the RQIA are currently making the appropriate arrangements to share the outcome of Phase Two and also to set out the next phase of the review process relating to deceased patients. The appropriate family engagement arrangements will be put in place to support the next steps, which will be confirmed in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will publish an update on the implementation of recommendations from the Independent Neurology Inquiry.
answer_text: The Department continues to work to ensure the Report of the Independent Neurology Inquiry (INI) receives the careful consideration it deserves, alongside putting in place the appropriate governance arrangements and work streams to deliver implementation of the INI recommendations. I intend to provide more detail on these arrangements in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to make the treatment Palforzia available to children here.
answer_text: Palforzia, for the treatment of peanut allergy in children and young people, was endorsed by my Department on 1st March 2022 and is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland in line with the current NICE guidelines (TA769). Further information on the treatment is available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta769

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his departmental officials have had any engagement with their counterparts in Britain regarding the planned Women’s Health Strategy and the potential impact on care locally, particularly the commitment to include miscarriage as an important health issue affecting women.

To ask the Minister of Health what additional funding is needed to ensure that the actions under priority 4 of the Stroke Action Plan, concerning rehabilitation and long term support, are implemented as planned.
answer_text: The programme of work outlined under Priority 4 requires an initial assessment of the gap between current services and the standard that we intend to build towards. Any funding requirements in respect of the actions required to close that gap will be identified upon completion of each assessment exercise. While I am determined to press ahead with stroke reform as far as possible, I have made my concerns clear about the impact on the pace of reform arising from the absence of budget certainty in the short, medium and long term.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is measuring the way in which providers of domiciliary care are using the funding they receive to stabilise and enhance capacity in that sector.
answer_text: The £23m funding package which I approved in November 2021, was intended to stabilise and enhance capacity within the independent domiciliary care and wider social care sector.The majority of funding was allocated against increasing the hourly rate paid, to a maximum of £18 per hour, for domiciliary care providers. This was with the expectation that providers would not only maintain service levels, but increase capacity. While the additional funding has allowed employers to offer enhanced rates of pay, and better terms and conditions, my Department has no remit to direct private companies as to the rate they pay their staff, or the terms and conditions that they offer. Officials from my Department continue to engage with Trust colleagues regularly, as well as independent sector providers, to monitor the impact that the funding has had, and indications are that there has been an overall increase in the hours of domiciliary care being delivered between November 2021 and March 2022.  Although the overall demand for care packages continues to increase, I am confident that the funding to date has assisted in stabilising the sector, and has increased capacity of providers to deliver care packages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has had any correspondence with the Public Health Agency or Health and Social Care Trusts on continued funding for the Diabetes Prevention programme.
answer_text: Prevention of Type 2 diabetes is one of the key areas in the Diabetes Strategic Framework for Northern Ireland, published in November 2016.  Diabetes prevention and the reduction of the number of people developing Type 2 diabetes is supported through the Diabetes Prevention Programme, with implementation through the Public Health Agency and in partnership with Health and Social Care Trusts. The Diabetes Prevention Programme is an evidence based, NICE compliant, lifestyle programme promoting behaviour change to delay or prevent Type 2 diabetes.  It is for adults who are defined by a blood test as having pre-diabetes and who are referred by primary care practices and willing to take part in group based sessions.The Diabetes Prevention Programme aims to support people to reduce their risk of developing Type 2 diabetes through weight reduction, healthy food choices and increased physical activity. The programme is facilitated by health coaches who offer help and assistance to participants, helping them change their lifestyle, diet and physical activity with the aim of postponing and even preventing Type 2 diabetes. The Diabetes Prevention Programme has been delivered virtually on-line during the pandemic, although this has been on a more limited basis due to staffing pressures.  There are waiting lists for the programme, which include people who have stated a preference for face to face participation.   Recruitment to the programme in four Trusts is currently paused due to funding challenges.  The Public Health Agency remains supportive of the programme and is considering how it can be funded in the longer term.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work his Department is undertaking to develop and publish a capability framework for Allied Health Professionals.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to develop a capability framework for AHPs in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many additional training places will be provided for Allied Health Professionals.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed budget for 2022-23 I have taken a decision for my Department to proceed at risk with opening allocations to continue to fund activity while controlling spending until such times as a budget can be agreed.I announced on 11 May 2022 a £2.1m education and training package to boost the healthcare workforce. This includes a one-off increase of 41 training posts on the pre-registration AHP programmes commissioned by my Department from Ulster University, commencing in September 2022. This increase is broken down as follows: Physiotherapy + 10Diagnostic Radiography + 10Occupational Therapy + 10Speech and Language Therapy + 5Podiatry + 3Dietetics + 3

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) vacancy; (ii) sickness; (iii) maternity; and (iv) long term leave rates for Allied Health Professions in each Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by specialism.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting lists for Allied Health Professionals for (i) children; and (ii) adults in each Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by specialism.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given any consideration to amending the rules governing the primary medical performers list to allow those qualified in the 26 counties ease of access to work in the Health Service.
answer_text: I am fully aware of the ongoing pressures facing General Practice across Northern Ireland.  My Department continues to work closely with GP representatives to consider how best to respond to these challenges. In doing so, my Department will consider a range of possible options to support the recruitment and retention of GPs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has taken exploratory action on a potential GP indemnity scheme similar to that in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: Work has recommenced to review arrangements for the provision of indemnity cover for GPs in Northern Ireland.  A range of options are under consideration from the status quo to alternatives such as the state backed systems adopted in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will ensure co-design and partnership working with service users, staff and other stakeholders will be central to the transformation of stroke services.
answer_text: My Department’s Reshaping Stroke Care consultation received in excess of 19,000 responses and over 1,600 people attended the seven public consultation events held. The voice of service users, carers, staff and other stakeholders have been central in developing the way forward for stroke reform in NI as set out in the recently published Stroke Action Plan. My Department is committed to building on that successful approach. In the context of the wide programme of reform set out in the Action Plan, I have asked my Officials to identify targeted opportunities for engagement as actions progress to ensure that the voice of service users, carers, staff and other stakeholders continues to inform the implementation of reform on the ground.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to Priority 4: Rehabilitation and Long Term Support of the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan, (i) how his Department will identify gaps in support for stroke survivors; (ii) what the Department's programme of work will entail; and (iii) how rehabilitation and long term support will be developed through co-design with patients and worker representatives.
answer_text: The actions linked to Priority 4 were identified following a review of evidence including the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis, the Stroke Association’s report ‘Struggling to Recover’ and the Long Term Support specification developed by the Stroke Network. It is intended that gaps will be identified through the programme of work outlined under Priority 4. This includes, for example, the identification of actions needed to ensure stroke survivors receive six month reviews and action needed to roll out Early Supported Discharge in each Trust. In addition, it is expected that planned work on spasticity services and pathways for referral to psychology and mental health services will identify gaps in those services. My Department is committed to working in partnership with stroke survivors and carers and will look for opportunities to do so throughout implementation of the Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will carry out equality and rural impact assessments when identifying and evaluating options for the establishment of hyperacute stroke care, as set out in the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan.
answer_text: My Department recognises the critical importance of understanding equality and rural impacts of any potential change in service. Consequently, consideration of such impacts will play a key role in identifying a preferred option for hyperacute stroke care in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what interventions he is making to (i) increase travel allowances for Health and Social Care staff, similar to interventions implemented in Scotland and Wales; and (ii) engage with care providers to deliver an uplift mileage allowance for care staff.
answer_text: In light of the recent significant increases in fuel costs, My Department, Employers and Trade Unions have been involved in national and local discussions regarding a potential review of the current Agenda for Change (AfC) Mileage Rates.The national discussions have been led through the NHS Staff Council and have been exploring the options in relation to mileage reimbursement rates. Unfortunately it has not been possible to conclude a national agreement on an increase in fuel reimbursement rates.In light of no nationally agreed position, temporary increases have been agreed in Scotland and Wales until the end of July and June respectively. At the time of those agreements, no decision could be made locally due to election period restrictions. My Department is, however, considering options for Northern Ireland.I have had regular engagement with local providers in relation to the range of issues facing the sector. This includes engagement via the Fair Work Forum, which I have established as a social partnership group committed to embedding Fair Work initiatives, and improving terms and conditions for everyone employed across all parts of the social care sector. The Fair Work Forum is currently developing a forward work plan to determine the priorities of the Group.Providers have raised concerns relating to staffing and capacity, but also the issue of increased fuel costs for domiciliary care workers who use their vehicle in the course of their day-to-day work. In recognition of the pressures facing the independent sector, on 24 November I announced a support package of up to £23m for the independent domiciliary and wider social care sector. This additional funding has been provided with the expectation that providers will not only maintain service levels, but increase capacity. Despite a very challenging financial position I have prioritised funding this year to continue supporting this sector.For domiciliary care workers employed by independent sector providers, there is no standard regional or set rate for mileage allowances, or towards the cost of vehicle upkeep. Individual providers, as employers, will offer a range of terms and conditions, including rates of pay and expenses, based on market conditions. While the Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions employers should offer, including in regards to mileage or travel costs, I have been clear that the additional funding must be used to stabilise and enhance capacity across the sector to see us through the current challenging period. The additional funding is being utilised by employers to offer enhanced rates of pay and better terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the number of adults who are waiting for speech and language therapy and for how long they have been waiting.
To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the number of adults who are waiting for occupational therapy and for how long they have been waiting.
To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the number of adults who are waiting for physiotherapy and for how long they have been waiting.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect the number of adults waiting for a physiotherapy assessment; therefore, the data was requested from each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many HSC Special Recognition Payments are outstanding; and when all the payments will be completed.
answer_text: I can confirm that as at 8th June 2022, a total of over £16.67m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,470 individuals. As at the same date over £3.82m has been paid out to 9,066 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £419k has been paid. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website: - https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment continues to be paid to independent sector staff as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what levels of cooperation exist between the two Monkeypox incident management teams that have been set up in the north and south of Ireland.
answer_text: The Chief Medical Officers and their teams in Northern Ireland and Ireland meet regularly. They have agreed to liaise and share information on the monkeypox response as required. Staff in the Public Health Agency and Ireland’s Health Service Executive Health Protection Surveillance Centre have statutory responsibilities for the health and wellbeing of the populations they serve.  Collaborative working and data sharing arrangements exist between the organisations and are maintained as part of any public health incident response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of (a) children; and (b) adults, who are waiting on a counselling appointment in primary care; and (ii) the length of time they have been waiting.
answer_text: This information is not collected centrally and is not available.  GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services.  The Department of Health’s newly formed Strategic Planning and Performance Group, formerly the Health and Social Care Board (HSCB), undertakes the functions of the HSCB, which officially closed on 31st March 2022.GPs are responsible for the day-to-day running of their practice, including waiting times for counselling appointments in primary care.
